title: Johnson & Johnson — Hematology — briefing Q1-2026
html:
What Changed in J&J Hematology — Q1 2026

Quarter: January–March 2026 Earnings reported: J&J April 14 | Sanofi April 24 | BMS April 24 | Pfizer April 29 (pending) Generated: 2026-04-27

1. TL;DR

  1. DARZALEX hit $4.0B in Q1 (+17.8%), with nearly 12-point frontline share gain — this single drug generates more revenue per quarter than most pharma companies generate per year.
  2. CARVYKTI reached $600M (+57.4%) — now a $2.4B run-rate product, growing via site expansion and becoming a legitimate franchise of its own.
  3. FDA approved TECVAYLI + DARZALEX FASPRO for 2L RRMM — the first bispecific-based combo approved as early as 2L, positioning TEC-DARA as a potential new standard of care.
  4. BMS's Abecma declined to ~$120M — manufacturing and safety challenges are creating a CARVYKTI-favorable narrative, while BMS pivots to next-gen CAR-T programs.
  5. Pfizer's Elrexfio remains small (~$60M est.) — Pfizer lacks the combination data J&J has with TEC-DARA; the 2L approval gap is now structural.

2. J&J Financial Signals

Product Q1 2026 YoY Growth Sequential
DARZALEX $4.0B +17.8% +$100M
CARVYKTI $600M +57.4% Strong
TECVAYLI ~$230M +30.1% +14.2%
TALVEY ~$210M +72.8% Strong
Total MM ~$5.04B — ~$20B annualized
  • TEC-DARA 2L approval is the franchise event: bispecifics move from 4L+ rescue to 2L standard option
  • Community expansion cited for both TECVAYLI (+14.2% sequential) and TALVEY (+72.8%) — the academic-to-community transition is happening
  • ZoomRx KPI tracker shows TEC-DARA at 52% physician appropriateness in 2L vs. CARVYKTI at 57% — segmentation, not winner-take-all

What J&J management said on the Q1 2026 call:

  1. "DARZALEX is entering its sustaining phase; growth will come from CARVYKTI and TEC-DARA" — this is a significant narrative shift. For the first time, J&J is framing DARZALEX as the stable foundation while positioning CARVYKTI and TEC-DARA as the growth engines. The portfolio maturation is being acknowledged publicly.
  2. Community expansion for bispecifics highlighted — management specifically cited community hematology adoption for both TECVAYLI and TALVEY. The academic-to-community transition signal is critical because community represents the volume opportunity.
  3. TEC-DARA 2L approval positioned as "paradigm shift" — J&J used this language explicitly for the first time, framing the approval as moving bispecifics from late-line rescue therapy to second-line standard of care. The regulatory milestone changes the commercial framing entirely.
  4. Raised FY2026 outlook to $100-101B — hematology (alongside immunology and FASPRO) was cited as a driver. The raised outlook provides quantitative confirmation of management confidence.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts noted DARZALEX growth deceleration (+17.8% vs. +24.1% in Q4) — the growth moderation was flagged, with J&J responding "Expected as we lap larger base; the franchise is healthy." The franchise health narrative is now being stress-tested by the Street as DARZALEX's growth rate normalizes.
  2. CARVYKTI manufacturing capacity remained a focus — consistent across three consecutive quarters, capacity is the dominant concern. The $600M quarterly number (+57.4%) shows strong demand but doesn't answer the question of how much higher it could go with unconstrained supply.
  3. TEC-DARA vs. CARVYKTI segmentation question evolved — analysts are now asking about patient segmentation data rather than just cannibalization risk. This is a positive shift — the conversation is moving from "will they cannibalize?" to "who gets which?" ZoomRx's 52% vs. 57% appropriateness data directly answers this question.

3. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q1 2026

Company Brand Q1 2026 YoY Growth Key Signal
J&J DARZALEX $4.0B +17.8% Frontline domination; anti-CD38 standard
J&J CARVYKTI $600M +57.4% Site expansion; preferred CAR-T
Sanofi Sarclisa ~$280M +20% est. Growing in combos but >14x smaller than DARZALEX
BMS Abecma ~$120M -15% est. Declining; manufacturing/CRS concerns
BMS Revlimid ~$900M -40% est. Generic erosion terminal; reshaping MM backbone economics
Pfizer Elrexfio ~$60M (pending) +50% est. Growing but no 2L combination data like TEC-DARA
AbbVie Imbruvica ~$600M -25% est. CLL declining to Brukinsa; not MM-relevant

Sanofi — Sarclisa (~$280M, +20%)

Sanofi Q1 2026 call (April 24, 2026):

  1. Sarclisa growing in combination regimens but structurally outmatched: Sarclisa (isatuximab) reached ~$280M in Q1, growing ~20%. Sanofi highlighted the IKEMA long-term follow-up (Sarclisa-Kd in 2L+) and IsKia (Sarclisa-KRd in NDMM) as growth drivers. But at 7% of DARZALEX's quarterly revenue, the scale gap is insurmountable.

  2. Sanofi positioning Sarclisa as "the other anti-CD38": Management acknowledged DARZALEX dominance in frontline but positioned Sarclisa as "the combination partner of choice in relapsed settings" — particularly for patients who had DARZALEX-based frontline therapy and need a different anti-CD38 backbone. This is a niche strategy, not a market leadership play.

  3. NDMM data (IsKia) is Sarclisa's best shot: The Phase 3 IsKia trial testing Sarclisa-KRd in transplant-ineligible NDMM is the one data package that could change the narrative. If it shows superiority or non-inferiority to DARZALEX-VRd, it gives Sarclisa a frontline foothold. Results expected 2026-2027.

Implication for J&J: Sarclisa is not a material threat to DARZALEX — the scale gap is too large and DARZALEX's data breadth (MAIA, PERSEUS, frontline dominance) is unmatched. The risk scenario is IsKia showing Sarclisa-KRd non-inferiority in NDMM, which would give payers a lower-cost alternative. Watch the data.

BMS — Abecma ($120M, declining) + Revlimid ($900M, terminal decline)

BMS Q1 2026 call (April 24, 2026):

  1. Abecma declining amid manufacturing and safety headwinds: BMS reported Abecma at ~$120M, down from ~$140M in Q1 2025. Management cited manufacturing capacity limitations and the FDA label update (boxed warning for T-cell malignancies post-CAR-T) as headwinds. CARVYKTI's smoother manufacturing scale-up and strong KarMMa-3 data are winning the physician preference battle.

  2. BMS explicitly acknowledged CARVYKTI's market leadership in CAR-T MM: In a notable concession, BMS management stated they are "repositioning Abecma within the broader portfolio" rather than trying to compete head-to-head with CARVYKTI for CAR-T market share. BMS is investing in next-generation CAR-T programs (including allogeneic and multi-target) rather than defending Abecma's position.

  3. Revlimid's generic erosion is reshaping MM economics: Revlimid declined ~40% to ~$900M as generic lenalidomide floods the market. This matters for the MM landscape because: (a) the cost of lenalidomide-based backbones drops dramatically, (b) payers become more receptive to expensive bispecifics/CAR-T if the backbone is now cheap, and (c) BMS's economic incentive to defend the old DARA-Rd paradigm disappears.

  4. BMS pivoting to next-gen hematology assets: BMS highlighted Opdivo+Yervoy (lymphoma), Breyanzi (CAR-T in LBCL), and early-pipeline programs. The MM space is increasingly conceded to J&J.

Implication for J&J: Abecma's decline is a tailwind for CARVYKTI — the CAR-T competitive dynamic is shifting from duopoly to J&J dominance. Revlimid genericization may paradoxically benefit DARZALEX-Rd combinations (cheaper backbone) but also opens the door for TEC-DARA to replace DARA-Rd at 2L. BMS's strategic retreat from MM creates opportunity for J&J to own the treatment algorithm.

Pfizer — Elrexfio (~$60M est., pending April 29)

Pfizer Q1 2026 earnings pending (April 29), but Elrexfio trajectory is clear:

  1. Elrexfio growing but constrained to late-line monotherapy: Elrexfio (elranatamab) is approved for 4L+ triple-class refractory MM. At ~$60M quarterly, it's growing (~50% YoY) but is structurally limited without combination data comparable to MajesTEC-3.

  2. No 2L combination approval — the defining competitive gap: J&J's TEC-DARA approval in 2L creates a structural advantage. Pfizer has MagnetisMM-7 (Elrexfio + DARZALEX) in development, but it's behind J&J's own combination data. The irony: Pfizer's best combination partner for Elrexfio may be J&J's DARZALEX.

  3. Pfizer's hematology attention split: Pfizer's oncology strategy is increasingly focused on ADCs (Padcev in bladder, seribantumab) and CDK4/6 (Ibrance lifecycle). Elrexfio receives modest promotional investment relative to J&J's bispecific franchise.

Implication for J&J: Elrexfio is a monitoring item, not a competitive threat. The 2L combination gap (TEC-DARA approved, Elrexfio monotherapy only) gives J&J a multi-year lead in the bispecific space. Watch for MagnetisMM-7 data — if it's strong, Pfizer could close the gap, but the timeline favors J&J.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. J&J's $20B annualized MM franchise has no credible challenger — DARZALEX ($4B/Q), CARVYKTI ($600M/Q), TECVAYLI ($230M/Q), TALVEY ($210M/Q) together dwarf the competition. Sarclisa ($280M), Abecma ($120M), and Elrexfio ($60M) combined are less than one-tenth of J&J's quarterly MM revenue.

  2. The competitive threat is internal, not external — the biggest risk to J&J's hematology franchise is portfolio cannibalization: does TEC-DARA in 2L reduce CARVYKTI utilization? ZoomRx data says no (only 6% shift from CARVYKTI to TEC-DARA), but this needs monitoring.

  3. Revlimid genericization creates structural tailwinds — cheap lenalidomide makes DARZALEX-Rd even more cost-effective for payers, and makes TEC-DARA look relatively affordable vs. old Kyprolis-based regimens. The MM cost curve is shifting in J&J's favor.

  4. Abecma's decline is CARVYKTI's gain — the CAR-T duopoly is becoming a monopoly. BMS has conceded. If CARVYKTI manufacturing scales further, the CAR-T market becomes a J&J franchise.

4. Clinical/Regulatory

  • TEC-DARA FDA approval (Q1 2026): First bispecific combo approved as early as 2L in RRMM — paradigm shift
  • MajesTEC-9: TECVAYLI monotherapy showed benefit in first relapse — expands the non-combo evidence base
  • CARVYKTI manufacturing expansion: New sites and improved slot throughput — capacity is the growth constraint
  • Sarclisa IsKia (Sanofi, Phase 3): Sarclisa-KRd in NDMM — the one trial that could give Sarclisa a frontline foothold
  • MagnetisMM-7 (Pfizer): Elrexfio + DARZALEX in RRMM — Pfizer's attempt to close the combination gap

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Evidence This Quarter
1. TEC-DARA reshapes 2L CONFIRMED — APPROVED FDA approval in hand; 52% physician appropriateness; shifting from DARA-triplets/Kyprolis
2. DARZALEX is the annuity CONFIRMED $4.0B Q1, 12-point frontline gain, Sarclisa at 7% of scale — no anti-CD38 competition
3. Bispecific vs CAR-T segmentation STRONGLY CONFIRMED 57% vs 52% split; patient segmentation emerging; Abecma declining removes the other CAR-T option
4. External competition is declining NEW — CONFIRMED Abecma -15%, Elrexfio small, Sarclisa niche; J&J's competitive risk is internal cannibalization
5. TALVEY underappreciated STRENGTHENED +72.8%, community expansion; 76% appropriateness in 4L+

6. What to Watch Next Quarter

  1. TEC-DARA 2L new start share — first full quarter of post-approval prescribing data
  2. Pfizer Elrexfio Q1 actual (April 29) — does it show any acceleration, or is the TEC-DARA gap widening?
  3. ASCO 2026 heme abstracts (late May) — MajesTEC-3 updated data, CARVYKTI expansion, TALVEY earlier-line
  4. Sarclisa IsKia readout timeline — the one external data event that matters
  5. CARVYKTI manufacturing capacity — any slot expansion affects the bispecific vs CAR-T dynamic

7. Conversation Starters for J&J

  1. "BMS conceded Abecma on their earnings call — they're 'repositioning within the portfolio.' CARVYKTI is becoming the de facto CAR-T in MM. But site capacity is your bottleneck. We should measure unmet CAR-T demand — how many patients want CARVYKTI but can't get a slot? That number drives your manufacturing investment case." — Uses competitor decline to frame J&J's growth constraint.

  2. "Your portfolio creates a sequencing dilemma: TEC-DARA in 2L vs CARVYKTI in 2L. Our KPI data shows 6% cannibalization — that's good news. But the segmentation is emerging: younger/fitter to CAR-T, older/high-risk to TEC-DARA. We can design a decision-tree study that maps this segmentation in real-time." — Highest-value BD pitch.

  3. "Revlimid going generic actually helps you — DARZALEX-Rd becomes cheaper for payers, and TEC-DARA looks relatively affordable vs. old Kyprolis triplets. The economic narrative is shifting in your favor. We should be tracking payer formulary changes as a leading indicator." — Synthesizing competitor economics into J&J insight.

Sources: J&J Q1 2026 (Apr 14), Sanofi Q1 2026 (Apr 24), BMS Q1 2026 (Apr 24). Pfizer Q1 2026 pending (Apr 29).

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
